title: Share, then make space
concept: Offer a small, relevant personal detail and leave room for the other person to respond or take the topic elsewhere.
skill_objective: User can contribute to common ground without turning a conversation into a monologue.

concept_intro.text: A small personal detail can make a shared topic feel real: it says, "I have been there too," without asking the other person to carry a long story. Keep the detail connected to what is already being discussed, then leave a little space. A related question, a pause, or an open ending lets the other person decide whether to add their own experience or move on.

example.setting: At a new-neighbor building event, Maya meets another resident, Leo, who also arrived in the building recently. They are talking about getting used to the neighborhood.

dialogue.speaker: Leo
dialogue.text: I moved in last month, so I am still learning where everything is.

dialogue.speaker: Maya
dialogue.text: Me too. I have enjoyed finding the little grocery shop around the corner; it made the area feel easier to navigate. Have you found a place that has helped you settle in?

dialogue.speaker: Leo
dialogue.text: The park has been useful for me. I walk there after work.

bad.text: I moved here last month too, and the first week was difficult because my boxes were late, my internet did not work, I had to change banks, and then I spent all weekend trying to find furniture.
bad.explanation: It begins with a relevant connection but becomes a long list. Leo has no clear opening to respond, and the shared topic has become Maya's account.

better.explanation: This creates a point of common ground, but it is so brief that it gives Leo little to respond to or build on.

best.text: I moved in recently too. I have enjoyed finding the small grocery shop nearby; it made the neighborhood feel more familiar. Have you found a place you like yet?
best.explanation: It offers one concrete, relevant detail and returns room to Leo with an optional, connected question. Leo can answer, add a different adjustment, or let the topic rest.

exercise.prompt: Another new resident says, “I am still getting used to this area.” Which reply shares enough while making space for them?

options.text: I know exactly what you mean. Let me tell you every problem I had during my first month here.
options.feedback: A long story can take over before the other resident has chosen how much they want to share.
This signals connection, but it gives the conversation little detail or direction.

options.text: I arrived recently too. Finding the library nearby has been one adjustment I have enjoyed. Has anything made the area easier for you?
options.feedback: Correct. It offers one small, relevant detail, then gives the other resident an easy choice to add their own experience.

practice.scenario_setup: At a building event, another resident says they also arrived recently. You want to mention one adjustment you have enjoyed, such as finding a nearby shop, park, bus route, or library.
practice.user_task: Draft a reply with one specific detail. Then compare it with an oversharing version and an undersharing version: keep the detail concise and add a question, pause, or open ending that leaves room for the other resident.

parts.question: A resident says, “I am still working out where to buy basic things around here.” Which reply has the best balance?

options.text: When I moved, I compared six supermarkets, had opinions about every aisle, and spent two weekends making a spreadsheet of prices.
options.feedback: This is much more detail than the moment needs and leaves little room for the other resident's experience.

options.text: I found a small market near the bus stop that I have liked so far. Have you found anywhere useful yet?
options.feedback: Correct. It contributes one useful detail and opens a connected, low-pressure space for the other resident to answer.

options.text: You should try the market near the bus stop.
options.feedback: Advice may be useful later, but this skips sharing a small personal experience and does not invite the resident into a mutual exchange.

parts.question: Which revision turns this overly long self-share into a concise contribution with space? “I have enjoyed the park, and I go there every morning, then I started listing all the paths, and my cousin visited, and we tried every cafe nearby...”

options.text: I have enjoyed the park nearby; it has become an easy place to take a walk. Have you found a spot you like?
options.feedback: Correct. It keeps one relevant detail, removes the extra history, and makes room for the other person to respond.

options.text: The park has many paths, three cafes, weekend events, and a long history that I can explain.
options.feedback: This is still a long information stream rather than a concise contribution.
This is concise, but it loses the personal, concrete detail that could create common ground.

parts.prompt: Revise this reply to a fellow new resident: “I moved recently too, and first I had to unpack for days, then I changed my phone plan, then I got lost on several buses, and my whole family had opinions about the move.” Write a concise contribution that mentions one adjustment you have enjoyed and leaves genuine space for them to respond or choose another topic.
parts.good_answer_demonstrates: One short, relevant personal detail about an adjustment the user has enjoyed, without a long list or monologue, followed by a connected optional question, pause, or open ending that makes room for the other resident.
